The specific answers are not important; but what is important is to know how to obtain the information. Possible answer: You would need to find an additional proportion. You would first find the "Total" number of students who do not play an instrument (18). Then, to find the proportion of those students who own a cat, you would look at how many students own a cat within that column (9) and divide it by the "Total" who "Does not play an instrument" (9/18).

  21. After a few minutes, ask a team to volunteer a response. Encourage students to agree or disagree with the explanations provided. Lead students to see that the total for the "Does not play instrument" column is important because we are now also concerned with that subset of the group. Mark up the "Does not play instrument" column on the table to show that we have conditioned, or are bound, by this variable. Compare the values that show the conditional relative frequency for each column, "Does not play instrument" and "Plays an instrument". A higher proportion of students who do not play an instrument own a cat than students who do play an instrument.

    Note: These are conditional relative frequency by column. We can also calculate conditional relative frequencies by row if we were interested in knowing the difference in playing an instrument for those who own cats versus those who do not.

  22. Was Rosa right? Answer: Looking back at our scenario introduced in the last lesson, "Rosa has a theory that musicians are more likely to own a cat," our analysis in steps 19-21 above does not provide evidence to support her theory. We found that a higher proportion of students who do not play an instrument own a cat than students who do play an instrument.

  23. Distribute one full set of cards from the Analyzing Categorical Variables file (LMR_U1_L17_A) to each student team.

    Advance preparation required: Print the Analyzing Categorical Variables file (LMR_U1_L17_A). The handouts can then be cut into a total of 20 cards (12 visuals, 8 numerical summaries). You will need enough sets of the cards for each student team to share one full set. For example, if there are 5 student teams in a class, then 5 copies of the file will need to be printed so that each team gets all 20 cards.

    LMR_U1_L17_A

  24. Distribute the Interpreting Categorical Variables LMR_U1_L17_B to each student team.

    LMR_U1_L17_B

  25. Each student team will work together and decide which visualization(s) and numerical summaries can be used to answer each statistical question. They will then answer each statistical question, citing a numerical summary as evidence.

    Note: Student teams may tape or glue visuals and numerical summaries onto LMR_U1_L17_B, or they can simply write the plot letter and table number in the appropriate box. The blank column is for student teams to write a statistical question that can be answered with a visual and a numerical summary that was not used.

  26. After student teams have been allotted ample time to complete LMR_U1_L17_B, lead a class discussion to go over the answers. It is extremely important to have students justify their answers by referring to their visuals and tables. For example, the statistical question “How many students neither own a cat or play an instrument?” can be answered with Plot E, Plot G, Plot I, Plot K, and with Tables 1 and 7.

  27. Ask students to refer back to the two-way frequency tables they created earlier. Have each team create one poster that shows their two-way frequency table. Then, ask each team to ask 5 questions about the data in their table that must be answered by a:

    1. marginal frequency

    2. marginal relative frequency

    3. joint frequency

    4. joint relative frequency

    5. conditional relative frequency (either by row or column)

  28. If time permits, pair teams up and ask them to present their findings to each other.

docs.title: Class Scribes:
docs.text: One team of students will give a brief talk to discuss what they think the 3 most important topics of the day were.

docs.title: Homework & Next Day
docs.text:
Using the data below, generate 2 questions: one must be answered with a marginal relative frequency and the other must be answered by a conditional relative frequency.

Work Preference and the Color Red

Which emotion do you most relate with the color red?

Love Anger Fear Total Morning shift 7 11 5 23 Evening shift 12 15 10 37 Total 19 26 15 60

Lab 1G: What’s the FREQ?

Complete Lab 1G prior to the Practicum.

docs.title: Objective:
docs.text: Students will be able to explain how different representations of data can tell different stories.

docs.title: Materials:
docs.text:
1. Candy Culprit Clues - The Case & Clue #1(LMR_U1_L02_A_Candy_Culprit_Clues)

  2. Locker Inventory List (LMR_U1_L02_B_Locker_Inventory_List)

  3. Detective Field Notes (LMR_U1_L02_C_Detective_Field_Notes)

  4. Evidence: Locker Photo (LMR_U1_L02_D_Evidence_Locker_Photo)

  5. OPTIONAL: T-Chart: Comparing Representations (LMR_U1_L02_E_T-Chart_Comparing_Representations)

docs.title: Vocabulary:
docs.text: representations

docs.title: Essential Concepts:
docs.text:
Essential Concepts:

Variables can be summarized using various representations, like lists, written notes, or photos. Different representations of the same variable can highlight different details and tell different stories. The choice of how to represent a variable or set of variables affects how we interpret the information. Looking at variables in multiple ways can lead to a more complete understanding.

docs.title: Lesson:Opening: Let the Case Begin!Concept DevelopmentClosing
docs.text:
1. Lesson Hook: Welcome the DSU detective recruits back to training. Inform them that the DSU has received a new case and the rookies have been tasked to investigate.

  2. Present the case by displaying the following on the board (LMR_U1_L02_A, page 1). Let the students know they will be investigating this case throughout all of Unit 1 and are expected to present their findings to the DSU Captain at the end.

    LMR_U1_L02_A

  3. Introduce the FIRST CLUE of our investigation! All of the clues can be found in the Candy Culprit Clues document (LMR_U1_L02_A, page 1).

  4. Explain that two Data Detectives were dispatched to the initial scene and have submitted their reports about the contents of the victim’s locker. Unfortunately, they provided different forms of evidence in their reports, and we have to make sense of them.

    1. The first detective prefers to organize their notes as structured data, so their observations are recorded as a bulleted list.

    2. The second detective likes to jot down their notes as unstructured data, so their observations are recorded as sentences in a story-like way.

    Part 1: Exploring and Drawing the Evidence

  5. Divide students into groups of 3 or 4.

  6. Explain that each group will receive one of two different representations of the evidence that was discovered at the student’s locker – either the first detective’s list or the second detective’s notes.

    1. Their mission is to use this evidence to “reconstruct the scene” of the candy crime by drawing a sketch of the locker.

    2. The drawing should be consistent with the data summary their team was provided.

    3. Emphasize that all groups will be provided with the EXACT same data, they are just in different formats.

  7. Distribute Evidence: Give half of the groups the Locker Inventory List (LMR_U1_L02_B) and the other half the Detective Field Notes (LMR_U1_L02_C). Both handouts include a blank locker drawing template on page 2 for the students to sketch their scenes.

    LMR_U1_L02_B

    LMR_U1_L02_C

  8. Launch the activity with the Pencils In the Middle strategy and allow students to observe and discuss their given representation with their group members.

    1. What do you notice?

    2. What do you wonder?

    3. What information can be gathered from your group's representation?

    STRATEGY:Pencils in the Middle

    • Set the problem: The teacher presents a problem or question for students to discuss.
    • Place pencils in the middle: Students take turns sharing their ideas or approaches to solving the problem. As they contribute, they place their pencils in the center of the table.
    • Ensure equal participation: A student cannot speak again until everyone in the group has placed their pencil in the middle to ensure all members have a chance to share their ideas.
    • Monitor and facilitate: The teacher can circulate to listen to discussions and, in some cases, pick up a random pencil and ask the student to share their idea with the teacher.
    • Begin the task: Once all pencils are in the middle and everyone understands the problem, students can retrieve their pencils and work independently on the task.

  9. Drawing the Scene: Task each group with drawing the “scene of the crime” based only on the evidence from their assigned representation. A generic drawing of a locker is provided on page 2 of both LMRs for students to use as a template.

    1. Remind students to think about how they can visually represent and arrange the items based on the information provided.

    2. Set a timer for 15-20 minutes for groups to complete their sketches.

    GROUP ROLES:The Evidence Collector

    • This student is responsible for carefully reading the inventory list or the detective's notes aloud to the group. They ensure all items and details are heard and confirmed by the team. They also double-check the evidence to make sure no crucial information is missed during the discussion
    The Lead Investigator
    • This student guides the group's interpretation of the evidence and ensures a collaborative, respectful environment. They facilitate discussions about where items should be placed, help the team make decisions about the drawing, and ensure everyone's ideas are considered as they piece together the scene.
    The Forensic Sketch Artist
    • This student is in charge of creating the physical drawing of the locker. They translate the group's collective decisions into the visual sketch, making sure the arrangement and details of the items accurately reflect the agreed-upon interpretation of the evidence
    The Operations Coordinator
    • This student acts as the team's project manager. They keep track of the time to ensure the group stays on schedule, manage materials, and are the designated person to ask the teacher questions if the group needs help or clarification. They also ensure the team's final output meets the task requirements.

    ADDITIONAL SUPPORT: Optional Object Cutouts/Stickers for Diverse Learners For students who struggle with drawing, provide simple cutouts or stickers that they can use to physically arrange the locker items on their handouts. Optional Virtual/Digital Version for Diverse Learners Provide an online locker photo template in Canva (or a similar product) that students can place graphics onto.

    Part 2: Uncovering the Clues

  10. Once the drawings are complete, have each group tape their drawing to a designated wall space around the room. There should be plenty of space between each drawing to accommodate the Gallery Walk in the next step.

  11. Gallery Walk: Instruct students to walk around the room and observe the drawings created by the other groups. As they walk around, they should look for and record any similarities and differences between the drawings, especially between the groups that received different types of evidence. Provide each student team with sticky notes, and instruct them to write one comment or question at each station they visit.

    STRATEGY: Gallery Walk Students walk around the classroom, as if in an art gallery, to look at different “stations” or “exhibits.” It promotes movement, collaboration, and a diversity of ideas. At each station, students work individually or in small groups to discuss, respond to, or analyze the content. They write their thoughts and reflections on sticky notes. After a set amount of time, the groups rotate to the next station.

  12. After the Gallery Walk, have students return to their desks.

  13. Lead a class-wide discussion using the following questions to help students connect the evidence and their observations from the Gallery Walk:

    1. First, focus only on the drawings of groups that were given the Locker Inventory List.

      1. Did every group with this representation draw exactly the same sketch? Sample answer: No. We had the same items, but didn’t put them in the same places.

      2. What was similar? Sample answer: We had the exact same items in total.

      3. What was different? Sample answer: We put the items in different spots in the locker.

    2. Second, focus only on the drawings of groups that were given the Detective Field Notes.

      1. Did every group with this representation draw exactly the same sketch? Sample answer: No. We had similar items, but they were different sizes and in different places.

      2. What was similar? Sample answer: We put a lot of the items in similar locations.

      3. What was different? Sample answer: We drew the textbooks standing upright, but other groups drew them laying down on top of each other.

    3. Compare the drawings from groups that received the Locker Inventory List with the drawings from groups that received the Detective Field Notes.

      1. Did every group draw exactly the same sketch? Sample answer: No. Although we had many of the same items in our drawings, we didn’t place them in the exact same places.

      2. What was similar? Sample answer: Most drawings had the same number of items.

      3. What was different? Sample answer: The textbooks were placed on different shelves or were in a different order.

      4. Which representation(s) resulted in sketches that were the MOST similar to each other? Answers will vary.

      5. Did the Detective Field Notes provide any information that the Locker Inventory List did not, and vice versa? Sample answer: Yes. The field notes gave more information about the placement of the items in the locker.

      6. Which representation do you think makes it easier to create a sketch from? Why? Answers will vary.

  14. Reveal the Evidence: Locker Photo (LMR_U1_L02_D) and explain that this is yet another representation of the crime scene.

    LMR_U1_L02_D

  15. Prompt another discussion to incorporate the photo representation. As students discuss the questions below, instruct them to create a T-Chart in their notebooks to record the advantages and disadvantages of each type of data representation. Sample answers are provided below. OPTIONAL: If preferred, a separate handout titled T-Chart: Comparing Representations (LMR_U1_L02_E) is available to distribute.

    LMR_U1_L02_E

    1. What information is present in the photo that was not explicitly included in either the Locker Inventory List or the Detective Field Notes? *Sample answer: We know the exact placement and orientation of each item in the locker. We can also see the color of each object now.

    2. What advantages and disadvantages are provided by using one representation over another?

      Advantages Disadvantages Inventory List Clarity and Precision: The inventory list provides a clear, itemized account of all the contents with exact counts. Ease of Comparison: It's simple to compare items directly, such as seeing that there is only one of each textbook. Easy to Replicate: It would be straightforward for a second detective to create an identical list. Lacks Context: The list doesn't show the spatial arrangement of the items. It can't show if the locker is messy or neat, or if an item is worn out. Hides Specifics: It doesn't reveal emotional or qualitative clues, such as the note's tone or the drawing's style. Field Notes Provides Context: The notes offer subjective observations and a narrative that isn't present in a list. Captures Early Impressions: The notes can convey a detective's initial thoughts and hypotheses, which can be valuable for an investigation. Includes Key Details: The notes can call out important pieces of evidence, like the cryptic note or the drawing, and offer an early interpretation. Lacks Structure: The information is often unorganized and may be difficult to sort or search. Prone to Bias: The notes reflect the detective's personal interpretations and can be subjective or miss important details. Not a Complete Record: The notes might only focus on what the detective thought was important, leaving out other details. Photo Unfiltered Truth: The photo provides a true, unfiltered visual record of the scene as it was found. Shows Relationships: It clearly shows the spatial relationships between all the items, which a list or notes cannot. Reveals Nuance: The photo can capture details like the condition of an item, the style of a drawing, or the look of a note. Requires Interpretation: The viewer must still interpret the image to make sense of the clues. Limited Scope: A single photo can only show one angle of the locker, potentially hiding other evidence.

    3. What story does each representation tell us?

    4. Remind students of the question that was posed when they were first introduced to The Case of the Candy Culprit, and then ask if they think they can answer the question after today’s exploration.

      1. Initial Question: Can you determine the identity of the Candy Culprit and bring them justice?

      2. At this point in the investigation, can we answer the question? Sample answer: No! We don't have enough information about who might be involved. We have only seen the crime scene.

      3. What do you feel you know about the Candy Culprit at this point? Sample answer: They might like chocolate candy since that is the piece that they took from the locker, but we can't be sure. Maybe they chose that locker because it wasn't locked and was easy to access.

    5. Wrap-Up: Allow 2 students to share one type of representation they saw today and how that impacted their ideas and assumptions about the crime scene. Sample answer: We learned about an inventory list, which is a structured way to look at data. We also explored some notes that a detective wrote while he was out at the scene, but it was unstructured and a bit messier. Each representation provided different clues and impacted the stories we created.

    6. Key Takeaway: The same evidence can be represented in multiple ways, but these different representations can have a major impact on how we perceive the “story” behind the data.

    7. Transition to next lesson: Announce that during their next day of training, the Data Detectives will be introduced to some students and staff from the school, and will start to see how these people fit into our story.

    8. Challenge students to come up with one positive aspect for each data representation type they interacted with today.

      1. Sample answer: The inventory list was good for specific details, like the exact number of pencils.

      2. Sample answer: The field notes provided a narrative story and made it easier to picture the locker.

      3. Sample answer: The photo gave us the visual truth.

docs.title: Objective:
docs.text: Students will take information from photos and organize them into groups by visual characteristics. They will practice making detailed observations to build a preliminary dossier on their suspects.

docs.title: Materials:
docs.text:
1. Guess Who? Online Game link: https://www.calculators.org/games/guess-who/

  2. Suspect Data Cards (See References for lesson inspiration)

    Advanced preparation required. See Class Setup section for additional details.

    1. Print Option(s) NOTE: Options B or C are recommended for Lesson 3. Option A is required for Lesson 4.

      • Option A: PDF document of Suspect Data Cards (FULL) - photo on left side; school information on right side: (LMR_U1_L03_A_Suspect_Data_Cards_FULL)

      • Option B: PDF document of left side of Suspect Data Cards (photos + names) with names included: (LMR_U1_L03_B_Suspect_Data_Cards_NAMES_PHOTOS)

      • Option C: PDF document of left side of Suspect Data Cards (photos only) with names excluded: (LMR_U1_L03_C_Suspect_Data_Cards_PHOTOS)

      • Digital Option(s) NOTE: Options E or F are recommended for Lesson 3. Option D is required for Lesson 4.

        • Option D: Canva whiteboard template of Suspect Data Cards (FULL) - photo on left side; school information on right side: (LMR_U1_L03_D_DIGITAL_Suspect_Data_Cards_FULL)

        • Option E: Canva whiteboard template of left side of Suspect Data Cards (photos + names) with names included: (LMR_U1_L03_E_DIGITAL_Suspect_Data_Cards_NAMES_PHOTOS)

        • Option F: Canva whiteboard template of left side of Suspect Data Cards (photos only) with names excluded: (LMR_U1_L03_F_DIGITAL_Suspect_Data_Cards_PHOTOS)

        • Data Cycle poster [pages 2 & 3] (LMR_U1_L01_B_The_Data_Cycle)

docs.title: Vocabulary:
docs.text: observations secondary data

docs.title: Essential Concepts:
docs.text:
Essential Concepts:

A core part of being a data scientist is to gather data by making careful observations. Organizing information based on observable traits helps uncover patterns and similarities. These observable traits are characteristics that can differ from one person to another.

docs.title: Lesson:Class SetupOpeningConcept DevelopmentClosing
docs.text:
• Advanced preparation required.

    • Prior to class starting, select whether your class will use the print or digital option of the Suspect Data Cards.

    • Print Option(s): [color printing recommended] Print and cut out enough sets of cards so that each group of 3 to 4 students gets their own set. As an example, if there are 20 students in the class and they are placed into groups of 4, you will need to prepare 5 sets of the Suspect Data Cards. The card sets can be stored in zipper bags or envelopes for easy distribution. NOTE: Only the left side of the FULL cards will be used for this lesson (the photo side). You can choose to print/cut/prepare just the left half of the cards for this lesson, OR print the entire card and fold it in half OR print/cut/prepare the ‘photos + names’ cards or the ‘photos only’ cards.

      • Option A (LMR_U1_L03_A): Suspect Data Cards (FULL)

        LMR_U1_L03_A

        • Option B (LMR_U1_L03_B): Suspect Data Cards (photos + names)

          LMR_U1_L03_B

          • Option C (LMR_U1_L03_C): Suspect Data Cards (photos only)

            LMR_U1_L03_C

          • Digital Option(s): 3 online Canva whiteboard templates have been created for you to copy. Each group would need access to their own whiteboard.

            • Option D (LMR_U1_L03_D): Canva whiteboard for Suspect Data Cards (FULL)

              • Option E (LMR_U1_L03_E): Canva whiteboard for Suspect Data Cards (photos + names)

                • Option F (LMR_U1_L03_F): Canva whiteboard for Suspect Data Cards (photos only)

                  1. Lesson Hook: Welcome the detectives back to training, and introduce the Guess Who? Online game.

                    1. Explain that the goal of the game is to guess which character the computer chose before it can guess which character we chose. The list is narrowed down by asking questions about the physical appearance of the characters. Play one round of the game (or 2, if time permits).

                    2. Once the play button is clicked, the website will prompt you to choose a character. There are 24 possible choices, so allow students time to observe the characters before selecting 1 as a class. Next, the website will prompt you to select a question to ask the computer player. The goal is to eliminate as many characters as possible during each round so you find the chosen character first. Some selected screenshots of progression through the game are provided here.

                  2. Welcome the DSU detective recruits back to training. Today's mission is to move from the crime scene to the suspects. Pose the following questions:

                    1. Do you have any ideas about who the Candy Culprit might be? Sample answer: No. So far, we have only looked at the crime scene evidence. We don't have any information about WHO might be involved.

                    2. What information might you need to start identifying people of interest? Sample answer: We could start with a list of all students and staff at the school. But there might be too many possible suspects, so maybe we can get a shorter list of people who were in the building at the time of the locker incident.

                  3. Bring back the Data Cycle: Display the current Data Cycle poster (LMR_U1_L01_B_The_Data_Cycle, page 2) on the board and remind students of the Consider Data phase they learned about in Lesson 1.

                    LMR_U1_L01_B

                    Part 1: Recording Observations

                  4. The school has provided the DSU with a list of all students, teachers, and support staff that have access to campus on any given day. Unfortunately, there are over 400 people on this list! Due to limited resources, there is no way the squad can investigate every single one of them.

                  5. Explain that our detectives will look through and analyze this data to build preliminary suspect dossiers. A dossier is a collection of documents about a particular person, event, or subject.

                  6. Ask: Is the data we are getting from the DSU an example of primary data? Why or why not? Sample answer: No. We did not collect the data ourselves. It is being provided to us by the DSU.

                    1. What do you think this type of data would be called?

                    2. Guide students to the understanding that this information already existed, and was gathered and recorded by the school. Because the data comes from someone other than ourselves, we call this secondary data.

                  7. The DSU Captain has decided to only investigate the people who were inside the building where the most recent candy heist occured. This narrows down our list of possible suspects to 30 people, including:

                    1. 21 students (7 - 6th graders, 7 - 7th graders, 7 - 8th graders)

                    2. 9 staff members (5 teachers, 1 administrator, and 3 support staff members)

                  8. The captain has confiscated the school ID cards of these 30 people and asked our Data Detectives to make observations from these cards in order to start creating a suspect dossier.

                  9. Place students into groups of 3 or 4 and hand out the Data Card sets (left side) OR provide a link to the Canva whiteboard template.

                  10. Students will be wearing their detective hats today as they examine the Data Cards. They need to identify any “clues” or information about the suspects that could give us some insight into a possible motive.

                    1. Emphasize that, for now, we only want to gather information that we can visually observe from the school ID photos. Provide the following questions to the detectives for them to consider as they record their findings:

                      1. What characteristics do the suspects have in common with each other?

                      2. In what ways are the suspects different?

                      3. Can these similarities and differences help us narrow down our suspect list at some point?

                      ADDITIONAL SUPPORT:Optional Modeling of an "Observation" for Diverse Learners Before students work in groups, model the process of observing a data card using the Fishbowl strategy, verbalizing the thought process by identifying different pieces of information (potential variables). Optional Sentence Starters for Diverse Learners "I observe that this person has (ex. eye color)." "A characteristic I see is , and its possible values are , , or ."

                      STRATEGY:Fishbowl A small group of students (the “fish”) sits in an inner circle and engages in a focused discussion on a specific topic. The rest of the class sits in an outer circle (the “observers” or “aquarium”) and listens silently, taking notes on the inner group’s conversation. Occasionally, the teacher may allow an empty chair in the inner circle, allowing an outer circle member to temporarily join the discussion before returning to the outer circle. The strategy encourages deep listening and focused discussion by a few, while allowing the many to observe and analyze the dialogue.

                    2. Encourage students to be creative and detailed with their initial observations. NOTE: There is no “correct” way to organize this initial information yet. We want our detectives to be creative and do not want to set rigid guidelines.

                    3. Whenever possible, they should record some actual numbers, or counts, for their observations. Some examples are below:

                      1. Example 1: There are 21 students and 9 adults.

                      2. Example 2: There are 7 students who are in the 7th grade.

                      3. Example 3: There are 7 people who are holding books in their photo.

                      4. Example 4: There are 2 people who wear glasses.

                      Enrichment or Extension:Counts Vs. Proportions Make a suggestion for students to calculate proportions as well as counts. Example: 7 of the suspects are 7th graders. Since there are 30 total suspects, 7/30 = 0.233, so 23.3% of the suspects are 7th grade students.

                  11. Whole Group Discussion: After teams have had time to discuss and record their findings, lead a class discussion about their visual observations.

                    1. What are some characteristics you observed about the people on the cards just from their photos? Sample answers are listed below:

                      1. Example 1: Grade level.

                      2. Example 2: Student or staff.

                      3. Example 3: Hair color.

                      4. Example 4: Item being held.

                      5. Example 5: Glasses or no glasses.

                    2. What are some characteristics you cannot observe from the pictures alone? Sample answers are listed below:

                      1. Example 1: Number of siblings.

                      2. Example 2: Travel method to school (bus, bike, car, etc.).

                      3. Example 3: Grade on the most recent math test.

                      4. Example 4: Favorite type of candy.

                      NOTE: It is important to think about who is and is not represented in these Data Cards. We may be assuming some information, but should be careful about how we do so. For example, recording a suspect’s “skin complexion” is likely better than recording an assumed “ethnicity,” but we should aim to record only the information that we know for certain based on the photos.

                    Part 2: Organizing Observations

                  12. Explain that a good detective knows how to organize evidence and then look for patterns. For the next task, students will physically group the data cards based on different observable characteristics.

                  13. Within their detective teams, challenge students to organize all 30 suspect cards into exactly 2 distinct groups based on a characteristic they observed from the photo.

                    1. If needed, guide teams with hints based on their written observations. Some example groupings are listed here:

                      1. Example 1: student (21) vs. staff member (9).

                      2. Example 2: wears glasses (2) vs. does not wear glasses (28).

                      3. Example 3: wearing a sports jersey (3) vs. not wearing a sports jersey (27).

                      4. Example 4: holding a book (7) vs. not holding a book (23).

                    2. Discussion: Once teams have chosen their criteria and physically placed their suspect cards into their 2 groups, allow each team to share out. Discuss if some criteria were easier or harder to apply consistently. Some example discussion ideas are listed here for reference:

                      1. Example 1 (simple): Grouping the suspects into students versus staff is fairly easy because the staff members have an icon that indicates "STAFF" as their role.

                      2. Example 2 (more complex): Grouping the suspects by their hair length is a more difficult task because we first have to agree on what it means to have "long hair." Some students might decide that "long hair" must go past a person's shoulders. Others might suggest that their classification of "long" versus "short" depends on a person's assumed gender.

                  14. Next, challenge the detective teams to reorganize the 30 suspect cards into exactly 3 distinct groups based on a different observed characteristic.

                    1. If needed, some example groupings are provided here:

                      1. Example 1: hairstyles - (1) hair is in a ponytail or braids, (2) person is wearing a hair accessory such as a hat or headband, (3) hair is worn down.

                      2. Example 2: shirt color - (1) red, (2) blue, (3) other.

                    2. Discussion: Once again, allow the detective teams to share the criteria they used to organize the suspects into 3 groups. Some discussion ideas are listed here for reference:

                      1. This is a good opportunity to bring up nuances. For example, organizing by “skin complexion” might come up.

                      2. Facilitate a discussion around how making clear, objective criteria with well-defined categories is important for consistent grouping. Categories like “skin complexion” can often be subjective and vary greatly.

                      3. If 2 teams chose the same observable characteristic (like hair style), did they select the same criteria for their 3 groupings?

                        1. If so, were the suspects that were placed in each grouping exactly the same?

                        2. If not, how were their groupings different?

                  15. If time permits, repeat Step 15 by challenging teams to reorganize the cards into 4, 5, or 6 distinct groups.

                  16. Connect back to the Case of the Candy Culprit:

                    1. Remind students about the empty candy wrapper (from a chocolate bar) and the note that was left in the student’s locker.

                    2. While we do not have enough information to fully use clues yet, prompt the detectives to start thinking: “Based on what we know so far, who might be someone of initial interest?”

                  17. Lesson Recap: Students made detailed observations about potential suspects based on school ID photos. They practiced organizing these observations into groups based on specific criteria.

                  18. Key Takeaway: Emphasize that organizing evidence in different ways helps detectives look for connections and patterns in data, even with just visual information.

                  19. Transition to the next lesson: Announce that more information about the suspects will be provided during the next lesson, and teams will be asked to create their own displays that appropriately organize it.

                  20. Exit Ticket: What do you suggest be the next step in your investigation and why?

docs.title: Objective:
docs.text: Students will be able to extract and organize additional information from the suspect data cards.

docs.title: Materials:
docs.text:
1. Suspect Data Cards (FULL)

    1. Print Option: (LMR_U1_L03_A_Suspect_Data_Cards_FULL)

    2. Digital Option: (LMR_U1_L03_D_DIGITAL_Suspect_Data_Cards_FULL)

  2. Large posters, whiteboards, or online collaborative tools (ex. Google Docs, Google Sheets, Canva, etc.)

  3. Markers

docs.title: Vocabulary:
docs.text: survey questions

docs.title: Essential Concepts:
docs.text:
Essential Concepts:

A successful investigation requires detectives to use all available evidence and clues. Data can be acquired in different ways, either through direct observation or by collecting specific information from individuals via survey questions. Organizing data systematically is crucial for finding patterns and managing a complex investigation.

docs.title: Lesson:Class SetupOpeningConcept DevelopmentClosing
docs.text:
• Advanced preparation required.

    • Prior to class starting, select whether your class will use the print or digital option of the Suspect Data Cards.

    • Note that the FULL version of the Suspect Data Cards is required for this lesson.

      • Print Option: (LMR_U1_L03_A_Suspect_Data_Cards_FULL) [color printing recommended] Print and cut out enough sets of cards so that each group of 3 to 4 students gets their own set. As an example, if there are 20 students in the class and they are placed into groups of 4, you will need to prepare 5 sets of the Suspect Data Cards. The card sets can be stored in zipper bags or envelopes for easy distribution.
      • Digital Option: (LMR_U1_L03_D_DIGITAL_Suspect_Data_Cards_FULL) A Canva whiteboard template has been created for you to copy. Each group would need access to their own whiteboard.

  1. Entrance Ticket: Have students answer the following 2 questions:

    1. What were 3 characteristics you observed and recorded from the Suspect Data Card photos in the previous lesson?

    2. Pictures can't tell us everything. What is one other piece of information you would want to know about a suspect, and how might a detective find that out?

  2. Recap that in Lesson 3, the detectives made initial observations about the suspects based on their school ID photos, and then identified some groups of people with similar visual characteristics. Today, they will be given even more information about the suspects by examining the full version of the school ID cards.

    Part 1: Creating Simplified Displays of New Evidence

  3. Place students into the same teams from Lesson 3 and distribute the prepared sets of the FULL version of the Suspect Data Cards OR provide a link to the Canva whiteboard template.

  4. Using the Pencils in the Middle strategy described in Lesson 2, instruct students to examine all the available information on the Suspect Data Cards and record some of their observations in their notebooks.

    1. They should notice that the left side is what they considered in Lesson 3 (the photos), but now they have a lot more written information on the right side about each person.

    2. Explain that the information provided on the right side of the cards was collected by asking survey questions, which they first learned about in Lesson 1.

      1. Challenge students to write some possible survey questions that would have generated the data that are on the cards.

    3. As the student detectives look at both sides of the data cards, ask them to note any differences in the types of information provided for different people.

      1. Does every data card contain the exact same information? Sample answer: no, but they do have a lot of similar information. For example, all of the cards list a “Favorite Candy”.

      2. More specific follow-up: Do all of the suspect data cards have GPAs listed? Do all cards have the number of years of service listed? What do you notice? Sample answer: GPA is only for students, and Years of Service is only for staff.

      ADDITIONAL SUPPORT:Optional Guided Exploration for Diverse Learners For teams that need a more structured start, explore ONE of the suspect data cards together. As a group, go piece by piece through the written information on the right side of the card. For each item (like Favorite Candy), ask:

      • "What survey question do you think the school asked to get this answer?"
      • "What does this piece of information tell us about this suspect?"
      This models how to extract and interpret the data before they start organizing.

  5. Explain that a core challenge during any investigation is managing a growing pile of evidence. Detectives need to simplify and organize all of the information so they can quickly analyze it, follow new leads, and present their findings to teammates, bosses, and sometimes even the courts. Our goal is efficiency in solving the case!

  6. Pose the following questions to prepare students for the next activity:

    1. Will it be convenient to keep all 30 Suspect Data Cards with you throughout the entire investigation? Sample answer: No. It would be difficult to sift through all 30 cards each time something new happens.

    2. Do you need to examine all 30 cards individually every time you receive a new piece of evidence? Sample answer: No. Once we have eliminated a suspect, we wouldn’t need to consider them as the Candy Culprit anymore.

    3. Is there a faster way to rule out suspects than by removing cards one-by-one from your suspect pile? Hint: Think back to the Guess Who? game at the beginning of class. Sample answer: Yes. We can have all the suspect photos and names put onto 1 page and cross them off as we eliminate them as suspects.

  7. Describe the next task for our detectives. In their teams, they will need to organize all of the data from the Suspect Data Cards and create a single, one-page display that will allow them (and others) to quickly access and view the information.

    1. Provide poster paper and markers or access to an online collaborative tool (ex. Google Docs, Google Slides, Canva, etc.) for students to create their displays.

    2. Additional instructions to guide students during the activity:

      1. When creating their displays, students should think like a lead detective preparing for a briefing. The display should allow them to quickly answer questions such as:

        1. Who are all the staff members?

        2. What is everyone's favorite class?

        3. Who has a high GPA?

        4. What activities are people involved in?

      2. Encourage creativity! There are no strict guidelines for how to complete this activity, but emphasize that their displays should be in an easy-to-understand format.

  8. As students work in their groups, circulate and guide as needed. You can prompt them with questions like:

    1. What's the most important information to put on your poster for our investigation?

    2. How can you make it easy to compare suspects?

    3. Is there a way to group similar information together like we did in Lesson 2?

    4. If you had to quickly find every suspect's favorite candy, how should you arrange the data to do that easily?

    ADDITIONAL SUPPORT:Optional Small-Scale Practice for Diverse Learners Instead of immediately jumping to the large poster with all 30 suspects, allow groups to first create a mini-display for just 3 to 5 suspects on a smaller piece of paper. This allows them to experiment with organization strategies on a lower-stakes task before tackling the full dossier. Optional Pre-Sorted Card Piles for Diverse Learners Have students physically sort the cards into two piles before recording any information (Example: (1) all staff cards, and (2) all student cards). Suggest they start by organizing the staff data first since there are only 9 of them.

  9. Allow ample time for the detective teams to create their displays. This activity will likely take up most of the class period.

    Part 2: Gallery Walk and Discussion

  10. Gallery Walk: Once teams have completed their posters, conduct a gallery walk around the room so they can observe how other teams chose to organize and present the suspect data.

    1. As they walk around, they should use their notebooks to record their observations about any similarities and differences between their displays.

    2. Additionally, provide teams with sticky notes and ask them to post constructive comments or questions for the other teams.

  11. Once students have returned to their seats, lead a discussion about the different layouts presented around the room. Pose the following questions to guide the discussion:

    1. Are some displays easier to understand than others? What makes them easy or hard to read?

    2. What are some characteristics of displays that you can understand quickly?

    3. Did anyone notice that some information was only on certain types of cards (ex. Students had GPA values, but staff members did not). How did your team decide to represent that on your display, or did you leave it out?

    4. Did any team organize the data in a way that looks like a table with rows and columns? NOTE: This is not the goal of this lesson, but it could be a nice segue into Lesson 5 where they will start considering how computers organize and understand data.

  12. Wrap-Up: Today, we moved beyond just visual observations and incorporated written data that was gathered from survey questions.

  13. Exit Ticket: What piece of information do you think will be most important to investigate to identify the Candy Culprit and why?

  14. Transition to the next lesson: Announce that we will learn an even more efficient and standardized way to organize our evidence, which will be crucial during the next stage of our investigation.

docs.title: Objective:
docs.text: Students will be able to transfer unstructured data into a structured data table, identify variables as column headers, understand the importance of consistent data entry, and begin to critically assess missing values in the context of investigative clues.

docs.title: Materials:
docs.text:
1. Suspect Data Cards (FULL)

    1. Print Option(s) (LMR_U1_L03_A_Suspect_Data_Cards_FULL)

    2. Digital Option(s) (LMR_U1_L03_D_DIGITAL_Suspect_Data_Cards_FULL)

  2. Team poster displays from Lesson 4

  3. Candy Culprit Clues [Clue #2] (LMR_U1_L02_A_Candy_Culprit_Clues)

  4. Data Table Template (LMR_U1_L05_A_Data_Table_Template)

  5. Candy Culprit Suspect Tracker (LMR_U1_L05_B_Suspect_Tracker)

docs.title: Vocabulary:
docs.text: data table case attributes variable missing value numerical variable categorical variable

docs.title: Essential Concepts:
docs.text:
Essential Concepts:

Organizing data into a structured data table, with rows and columns, is a powerful way to make information easy to search for and analyze. Each column in a data table represents a variable, which is a characteristic that can change or vary for each individual. In real-world data, missing values are common and must be handled carefully. An absence of information does not necessarily provide evidence in one direction or the other.

docs.title: Lesson:OpeningConcept DevelopmentClosing
docs.text:
1. Have all of the poster displays from the previous lesson visible at the front of the classroom. Lead a brief discussion reflecting on the activity and Gallery Walk. Pose these questions:

    1. Which of the displays made it easiest to quickly find a suspect’s information? Why do you think so?

    2. If needed: What organizational structure did the most efficient displays share with each other?

    3. Which of the displays would be easier for a computer to understand and interpret? What are some characteristics of these displays?

  2. Introduce the SECOND CLUE of the Candy Culprit investigation. All of the clues can be found in the Candy Culprit Clues document (LMR_U1_L02_A).

    LMR_U1_L02_A

  3. Initial Discussion for Clue #2:

    1. What new piece of evidence does this note force us to look at? Sample answer: GPA.

    2. What might this mean for our investigation? Sample answer: The culprit could be a student, or a staff member trying to throw us off the trail by mentioning a student-specific data point.

      ADDITIONAL SUPPORT:Prompting Question for Diverse Learners Based on what we know about the suspects from the cards, who even has a GPA? Sample answer: Only the students have GPAs listed. Staff members do not.

    Part 1: Data Tables, Cases, and Variables

  4. Explain to the student detectives that they are going to continue exploring the Suspect Data Cards, but need to level up how they organize the data. Instead of creating abstract representations, we now want to consider a more standardized and universal way to look at data. This is a crucial step for evidence management during any data investigation.

  5. Draw the following diagram on the board and ask students how it might have been used to organize our Suspect Card data in a standard way. If any of the team displays from Lesson 4 resemble a table with rows and columns, use that as an example of what a standardized display could look like.

  6. Introduce the terms data table, case, attributes, and variable one at a time. For each term, students should record the term, its definition, and its corresponding diagram.

    1. Data Table: A collection of organized information presented in rows and columns. Rows are organized horizontally and columns are organized vertically.

      Column #1 Column #2 Column #3 Row #1 Row #2 Row #3

    2. What goes in each row? A case. A case is an individual person, thing, or event we are observing and collecting data about. In a data table, every row represents one case. So, in our Candy Culprit investigation, each suspect is a case.

      Column #1 Column #2 Column #3 Case #1 Case #2 Case #3

    3. What goes in each column? Attributes, or characteristics, about the suspects, like their "Favorite Candy." In data science, we call these attributes variables. Point out that the root word of variable is "vary". Ask a volunteer to share what they think this term means. You can guide the discussion with the following questions:

      1. What does it mean for something to vary? Sample answer: When a value can change from one observation to the next.

      2. Did the observations you recorded for the Suspect Data Cards vary? Give an example. Sample answer: Yes, students wore different colored shirts, so it changed from person to person.

      3. What are some synonyms for vary? Sample answer: different, differ, change, contrast, alter, etc.

      4. Next, explain that variables are what we call those groups of characteristics, or attributes, that can change from person to person. We call them variables because their values vary from row to row.

      Variable #1 Variable #2 Variable #3 Row #1 Row #2 Row #3

  7. Using the same student teams from Lesson 4, engage students in a Variable Brainstorm activity.

    1. Ask a student volunteer to suggest one variable their team might have observed from the data cards, and list the different values the variable could take. Sample answers: (1) “Favorite Candy” could be a variable because each person’s favorite candy can vary among Chocolate, Chewy/Gummy, or Hard/Lollipop. (2) “Age” could be a variable because the values vary between 11 and 60.

    2. Team Task: In their groups, ask students to come up with and record a list of possible variables that they collected from the Data Cards. Guide students back to their observations from Lessons 3 and 4 if they need extra support. NOTE: Teams do not need to find every possible variable, but they should identify at least 3 different ones.

    3. Once each team has written down their list of variables, ask them to decide on one variable that is of interest to them. Record the choices on the board for easy reference.

  8. Distribute the Data Table Template (LMR_U1_L05_A) to students and guide them through how to set up the first column of the Suspect Data Table using the variable they chose in Step 6. Then, have them select 6 Suspect Data Cards and record the values for their chosen variable.

    LMR_U1_L05_A

  9. Using the list of variables that other teams came up with, ask students to add more columns to their data table. As a hint, you can tell them that the final data table will include 11 variables. Can they name them all?

    1. Name

    2. School ID #

    3. Grade

    4. Age

    5. Favorite Class

    6. GPA

    7. Favorite Candy

    8. Activities

    9. Library Books Checked Out – This is a good opportunity to discuss the idea of concise labels in a data table; we might shorten this variable name to just “Books.”

    10. Years at School

    11. Role (student or staff) – This variable might not be as obvious, but it makes sense to include so we can distinguish between students and staff members.

    Enrichment or Extension:Common Guidelines for Assigning Variable Names in Datasets

    • Avoid using spaces. Instead, use an underscore symbol (_) or mixed-case capitals. AVOID: Student Name, student name TRY: Student_Name, student_name, studentName, StudentName
    • Include units of measurement when appropriate. AVOID: distance, height TRY: distance_km, distanceMiles, height_cm, heightInches
    • Aim for clarity over brevity. AVOID: years TRY: yearsInSchool, yearsWorking

  10. Have students complete the entire row of data for Aaron Appleby.

    a. Name: Aaron Appleby b. School ID #: 7215 c. Grade: 7th d. Age: 13 e. Favorite Class: English f. GPA: 3.8 g. Favorite Candy: Hard/Lollipop h. Activities: Student Council i. Books: 35 j. Years at School: [leave blank] k. Role: Student

  11. Then, have students complete the entire row of data for Anita Dayhoff.

    a. Name: Anita Dayhoff b. School ID #: 0648 c. Grade: [leave blank] d. Age: 60 e. Favorite Class: English f. GPA: [leave blank] g. Favorite Candy: Hard/Lollipop h. Activities: Student Council i. Books: 5 j. Years at School: 30 k. Role: Staff

    Part 2: What To Do When Evidence is Missing

  12. Engage students in a discussion about the places in the data table that they left blank.

    1. Are there any variables in the student cards that are NOT in the staff cards? What are they? Sample answer: Yes. Students have Grade and GPA, but the staff do not.

    2. Are there any variables in the staff cards that are not in the student cards? What are they? Sample answer: Yes. Staff have Years at School, but the students do not.

  13. Formally introduce the concept of missing values. In real investigations, detectives often encounter missing values, or places where information should be, but isn't. It could mean that this data does not exist, or simply was not collected.

    1. Refer students back to our newest clue that mentions a “high GPA” and ask:

      1. Can we complete all 30 rows of our data table using the variable GPA? Why or why not? Sample answer: No, we can only complete GPA values for students.

      2. If a suspect doesn’t have a GPA value, what should we write in that cell of the data table? Sample answer: We can just leave it blank, or we can maybe put a “–” symbol in its place.

      3. How does a real-life detective handle missing information when investigating a crime? Sample answer: They look for other clues, but keep people on the suspect list until a new piece of information comes to light.

      4. Does the absence of a GPA for staff members mean they are automatically innocent and we can eliminate them from our suspect list? Sample answer: No! This is a crucial point in real investigations.

  14. Explain that, in the context of the Candy Culprit investigation, when a piece of data about one of the suspects is missing, it doesn't automatically mean that suspect is innocent. It just means we don't have that specific piece of evidence for them. This is a crucial concept in real investigations. We can’t eliminate any staff suspects based on Clue #2 because missing data, in this case, does not equal innocence.

  15. Distribute the Candy Culprit Suspect Tracker (LMR_U1_L05_B) and explain that we will be using this handout for the rest of the unit to keep track of who we think might be the Candy Culprit. Ask:

    1. Who can be eliminated at this point in our investigation? Answers will vary, but this is a great opportunity to discuss the ambiguity behind the phrase “high GPA.”

    LMR_U1_L05_B

    Part 3: Classifying Variables as Categorical or Numerical

  16. Direct students back to the data table and ask them to participate in a Think-Pair-Share activity about the actual values they see for each variable. They should answer the following questions in their notebooks:

    1. What type of information did you input in Column #1? Was it words or text? Was it a numerical value?

    2. What values did you observe for the Favorite Class variable? Sample answer: Words like English, Math, and Social Studies.

    3. What values did you observe for the Books variable? Sample answer: Numbers like 1, 5, and 35.

  17. Next, explain that in data science, variables are classified into 2 groups based on their values. Write the following terms on the board and ask student volunteers to add their own definitions to them.

    1. Numerical Variables: data that can be expressed as numbers that come from a measurement or count.

      1. Another way to think about it: we can use arithmetic operations on these numbers and the results make sense (ex. Adding up all the values from the Books variable tells us there were #X of books read).

      2. FUN FACT!

        1. Not all numbers are numerical! Sometimes, numbers can act as codes that translate to categories. For example, the Grade variable from earlier could have used “sixth” or “6th” instead of the number “6” to denote that a student was in the sixth grade.

        2. It typically would not make sense to do arithmetic operations with this variable. Adding up all the 6’s in the Grade column wouldn’t mean that there are 36 Grade levels.

      3. Categorical Variables: data that can be expressed in distinct, non-numerical categories. Instead of numbers, we see labels or categories.

        1. Another way to think about it: we can use words to describe specific characteristics or qualities about something.

      ADDITIONAL SUPPORT: Create an Anchor Chart for Vocabulary Terms Keep the Numerical and Categorial variable definitions displayed. Use icons or letters to help students distinguish between the two variable types:      Ex. Use a number sign (#️⃣ or #) or the letter N for Numerical.      Ex. Use an ABC block (🔠) or the letter C for categorical

  18. Based on these new definitions, ask students to place the letter N or C above each variable name in the data table based on which variable type they think it is. After they have made their decisions for all 11 variables, share the actual classifications.

    1. Numerical Variables: Age, GPA, Books, Years at School

    2. Categorical Variables: Name, School ID #, Grade, Favorite Class, Favorite Candy, Activities, Role

  19. Final Task: Students must input all of the data from the 30 Suspect Cards into their data tables.

  20. Key Takeaway: Review that the data table is the official DSU format for all evidence. It standardizes the cases (rows) and variables (columns) and immediately highlights where missing values exist, which prevents false assumptions.

  21. Exit Ticket: Students will submit their answers to the question, “If the next Candy Culprit clue is about the variable Years at School, will we be able to eliminate any students? Why or why not? Use the vocabulary words case, variable, and missing value in your answer.”

  22. Transition: Announce that in the next lesson, the detectives will learn how to enter this neatly structured data into a digital Detective Toolkit (a specialized computer program) to begin the actual statistical analysis.

docs.title: Objective:
docs.text: Students will be able to navigate the CODAP environment, create variables (columns) and enter cases (rows), and understand the difference between the case-card view and the case-table view. They will also be able to verify that CODAP correctly recognizes categorical and numerical variables.

docs.title: Materials:
docs.text:
1. Computers/tablets with internet access

  2. Data Table Template (LMR_U1_L05_A_Data_Table_Template) - completed during Lesson 5

  3. CODAP website

  4. Link to a Blank CODAP File (optional)

  5. Link to the CODAP Candy Culprit Suspect Data File

docs.title: Vocabulary:
docs.text: CODAP case card case table

docs.title: Essential Concepts:
docs.text:
Essential Concepts:

Data analysis software tools expect data in a particular format, usually the case-attribute format discussed in the previous lesson. However, these tools often make mistakes when reading a data file, and so we must ensure the tool correctly identifies variable types (categorical vs. numerical) to prevent errors in subsequent analysis.

docs.title: Lesson:OpeningConcept DevelopmentClosing
docs.text:
1. Review: Briefly recap the importance of data tables from the previous lesson.

    1. Case tables are standardized ways to view datasets. We sometimes refer to them as simply data tables.

    2. The rows represent cases.

    3. The columns represent variables.

  2. Lesson Hook: Transitioning from Paper to Pixels

    1. Using the case tables they created by hand during Lesson 5, ask students to determine the answer to the question, “How many suspects have Chocolate listed as their favorite candy type?” Answer: 9 suspects.

    2. Point out that humans can be quite slow to answer the question because it takes time to scan through all the rows and columns by hand. Ask:

      1. Our data table only had 30 total cases, but what if there were 1000 cases? How long do you think it would take to answer the same question? Sample answer: Much longer, especially if the values were not sorted alphabetically.

      2. What tool could we use to find the answer faster? Sample answer: A computer.

      3. How might moving the data into a computer program make solving cases easier? Sample answer: It can help us sort, filter, and count cases quickly.

  3. Explain that the DSU needs to solve cases quickly and efficiently since resources are limited, so today the detectives will learn how to use CODAP (Common Online Data Analysis Platform) as part of our detective toolkit.

    Part 1: Hands-On Data Entry

  4. Introduce students to the CODAP website and its interface.

    1. Guide students to the CODAP website and briefly explain that it will become an essential part of our detective toolkit.

    2. Direct students to open a Blank CODAP File or have them click on the orange "Launch CODAP" button in the top-right corner of the screen.

    3. Instruct students to select the "CREATE NEW DOCUMENT" button in the middle of the screen. This will create an empty workspace in CODAP.

    4. Instruct them to click the "Tables" icon in the top-left corner of the toolbar to create a New Data Set. Then, select "--new--" from the dropdown menu. Once an empty data table opens, rename it to "Candy Culprit Suspects." Note that CODAP typically refers to a data table as a case table.

  5. Next, guide students to start building the structure of the data table by adding columns for each variable.

    1. Rename the first column by clicking on the label "AttributeName" and then selecting the "Rename" option from the dropdown menu. Type in a short and precise variable name, such as "Name".

    2. Once all students have successfully renamed the first column, instruct them to use the grey plus icon in the top-right corner to add and then rename the other 10 columns with appropriate variable names.

  6. Guide students to continue building the structure of the data table by adding their first case in row 1. They should use their hand-written data table from the previous lesson as a guide.

  7. Allow students to enter the data from 5 of the suspects into the data table. They should aim to include one student per grade level and at least one staff member An example is provided here:

  8. Once students have 5 suspects, lead a whole-class discussion about the differences they noticed between writing the data into the paper data table versus typing the data into the digital data table. Guide the discussion with the following questions:

    1. What challenges did you face when trying to set up your variables (columns)? *Sample answer: Making sure I typed the names correctly; making sure I didn't mix my numerical data with my categorical data.

    2. If a suspect did not have a value for a particular variable, what did you type into the data table? *Sample answer: If a piece of data was missing for a particular suspect, I did not type anything into that cell of the data table. I just left it blank.

      ADDITIONAL SUPPORT:Scribe Support for Diverse Learners For students who have difficulty with typing speed or fine motor control, pair them with another student for support. Designate one student as the “Coder” (types on the keyboard) and the other as the “Data Caller” (reads the paper data aloud).

    Part 2: Variable Integrity Check

  9. Introduce the importance of data integrity when entering values into a data table. Explain that CODAP is smart, but it can make mistakes when classifying variables. It is the detective's job to ensure that each column is correctly marked as categorical or numerical.

    1. Show students how to check the variable classification for at least one of the columns. Click on the label for one of the variables, and then select the “Edit Attribute Properties…” option from the dropdown menu.

    2. When the new pop-up window appears, click on the dropdown menu for “type” and select the appropriate classification. 2 examples are included below (1 categorical, 1 numerical).

  10. Give students time to check and modify the variable type for all 11 columns.

    Part 3: Navigating the Full Dossier

  11. The Official Data File: Tell the students they have just demonstrated their ability to structure and organize data, so the DSU is now trusting them with the full, standardized file of all 30 suspects.

    1. Have students save and close their 5-suspect CODAP files.

    2. Share the link to the Official CODAP Suspect Data File and instruct students to open and save it to their computer or Google Drive account.

    3. The data table (or case table) should automatically load in the CODAP window.

    4. Show students that CODAP offers 2 different ways to view data. The first is the case table view, and the second is the case card view.

      1. In order to switch between the views, click the white menu button in the top left corner of the case table.

      2. A prompt will pop up that says “Switch to case card view of the data.” Click the prompt.

      3. The case card view is now visible. The very first card is a bit different from the actual suspect cards, so allow students to explore this view by using the left and right arrow buttons next to the “add case” button to navigate through the cards. Students should record how the first case card differs from the case card for Aaron Appleby.

  12. Allow students to partner up for a Think-Ink-Pair-Share activity to discuss the following questions:

    1. If you needed to check every single variable for one suspect, which view is better? Answer: The Case Card view because it shows one suspect, or case, at a time.

    2. If you needed to compare all 30 suspects’ GPAs at once, which view is better? Answer: The Case Table view because we can see many of the cases at once.

  13. Revisit the question posed during Step 2 of the Opening section of this lesson (“How many suspects have Chocolate listed as their favorite candy type?”). Can we answer this question easily with CODAP? Which view would make it easier to answer this question?

    1. Explain that CODAP makes organizing our data very easy by allowing us to sort by one variable at a time.

    2. In the case table view, click the variable name “FaveCandy” and have students observe the drop-down options. What option(s) would help us find the chocolate lovers quickly? Answer: Sort Ascending (A -> Z, 0 -> 9) or Sort Descending (9 -> 0, Z -> A). Ascending might be best here, though, because “Chocolate” starts with the letter C and is near the beginning of the alphabet.

    3. The screenshot below shows the top of the sorted data table.

      1. Ask students to quickly determine how many suspects chose “Chocolate” as their favorite candy. Does this answer match what they found during our opening activity? Answer: There are 9 suspects whose favorite candy is Chocolate, which matches what we said earlier.

      2. Which method made finding the total number of chocolate lovers easier – counting by hand or sorting in CODAP? Sample answer: Sorting in CODAP is easier and faster because it groups all of the suspects with a value of “Chocolate” together.

  14. Quick Practice: Direct students to review Clue #2 from Lesson 5 and allow them to use their new CODAP skills to reexamine the GPA variable. They should record how the results either confirm or dispute their findings from Lesson 4. Sample answer: When GPA is sorted in ascending order, the first 9 cases have missing values, and we can see that those cases are all of the staff members. We still cannot eliminate any staff members based on their GPAs because missing data does not imply innocence.

  15. Key Takeaway: CODAP is a powerful tool that helps us view and organize data quickly.

  16. Transition: Announce that in the next lesson, students will learn how to use data tables to help us ask better investigative questions.

  17. Exit Ticket: Explain one thing the Case Table view is better for, and one thing the Case Card view is better for.

docs.title: Objective:
docs.text: Students will be able to differentiate between statistical questions and survey questions. They will practice formulating both types of questions and understand the characteristics of good statistical questions. Students will be introduced to the Pose Questions phase of the Data Cycle.

docs.title: Materials:
docs.text:
1. Data Cycle poster [pages 2 & 3] (LMR_U1_L01_B_The_Data_Cycle)

  2. Statistical Questions Peer Review (LMR_U1_L07_A_Statistical_Questions_Peer_Review)

docs.title: Vocabulary:
docs.text: Pose Questions (phase) statistical questions

docs.title: Essential Concepts:
docs.text:
Essential Concepts:

Good questions are the starting point for any data investigation. The type of question asked determines the type of data needed and the scope of the investigation.

docs.title: Lesson:OpeningConcept DevelopmentClosing
docs.text:
1. Lesson Hook: Quick Collection Challenge

    1. Pose a simple question to students and ask them to come up with a single response based on their own experience. Suggested question: How many different states in the US have you visited?

    2. You will ask students to give their answers quickly using one of two visual methods: (1) Stand and Share, or (2) Four Corners.

      1. Method 1: Stand and Share – Ask for groups to stand up based on their answer. Quickly call out the ranges and tally the results on the board.

        1. Ask “Who has been to 1 or 2 US states?” (Count hands)

        2. Ask “Who has been to 3 or 4 US states?” (Count hands)

        3. Ask “Who has been to 5 or 6 US states?” (Count hands)

        4. Ask “Who has been to more than 6 US states?” (Count hands)

      2. Method 2: Four Corners – Have locations in the room for the various responses and have students move to that part of the room.

    3. Quick Discussion: Point to the collected counts on the board and ask, “Did everyone give the same answer to my question?” The answer will very likely be no.

      1. We have a lot of different answers, which means we have variation!

      2. We needed to collect data from each student in our class to see how we are similar and/or different.

      3. Explain that today’s lesson will help us identify and create questions that have many answers

  2. Display the Data Cycle poster with only the Consider Data phase shown (LMR_U1_L01_B, page 2). Have students share out some of the ways we have considered data so far in our investigation.

    LMR_U1_L01_B

  3. Reveal and highlight today’s phase of interest: Pose Questions (LMR_U1_L01_B, page 3), and explain that, like any good detective, the DSU needs a clear direction. Asking an investigative question is the first step to finding a pattern. Today, we officially begin the Pose Questions phase of the Data Cycle.

  4. Remind students that they learned about Survey Questions during Lesson 1, and pose the following questions:

    1. What were some of the questions that were asked at each station?

      1. Sample answer: What is your favorite type of candy?

      2. Sample answer: How many books did you read over the summer?

    2. Were those questions addressed to the class as a whole group, or were they addressed to individual students? Sample answer: Students answered the questions individually.

  5. Emphasize that survey questions are posed to individuals, and each person provides 1 answer.

  6. Explain that today, we're going to explore a different type of question we can ask when we want to learn about groups of people.

    Part 1: Introducing Statistical Questions

  7. Inform students that good detectives know that asking the right questions is one of the most important steps when solving a case. In a statistical investigation, we call these Statistical Questions. Share the definition below and have students record it in their notebooks.

    1. Statistical Question: A Statistical Question is a question that leads to many different answers when you ask an entire group of people or measure a group of things. The key feature is that the answer varies (or changes) for each person or item in the group. Statistical questions are all about figuring out the patterns and characteristics of a whole group by measuring this variation.

    2. Point out that having variability in answers is what makes a question statistical. A good way to determine if a question is a statistical question is to ask: “Do I need data from multiple individuals to answer this question?”

  8. Display the following 2 questions on the board:

    1. Question A: What is your favorite class? [Think about if you were Max from the suspect data cards; how would he answer this question?]

    2. Question B: What is the favorite class of the suspects in the DSU data set?

  9. Engage students in a Think-Ink-Pair-Share discussion about the differences they notice between Question A and Question B. Some guiding questions are provided here, and sample answers are given in the next step.

    1. Who would you ask each question to?

    2. What kind of answer would you expect?

    3. How many responses would you need to collect to fully answer the question?

  10. After pairs have discussed, bring the class back together and have a few volunteers share what differences they identified. Sample answers and ideas are provided below:

    1. Question A:

      1. Sample answer: Question A is asked about one person.

      2. Sample answer: Question A has one answer (Max’s favorite class is Social Studies).

    2. Question B:

      1. Sample answer: Question B is about the entire suspect list.

      2. Sample answer: Question B needs many answers to figure out a consensus on which class everyone likes.

      3. Sample answer: For Question B, we would expect different suspects to have different favorite classes (variability). We need many responses to come up with an answer.

      4. Sample answer: Question B might have a summary answer (“most suspects like X class”).

  11. As students share out their findings, record some of the key differences on the board and have students do the same in their notebooks.

  12. Explain that Question A and Question B are examples of Survey Questions and Statistical Questions. Ask students to make an educated guess about which question type matches Question A and which type matches Question B. Answer: Question A is a survey question and Question B is a statistical question.

  13. Solidify the difference between survey questions and statistical questions by connecting them back to what the students observed during the Think-Ink-Pair-Share activity.

    ADDITIONAL SUPPORT: Add to the Anchor Chart for Vocabulary Terms Keep clear definitions and examples of Survey Questions and Statistical Questions displayed throughout the lesson. Use icons or simple drawings to help students distinguish between the 2 question types:      Ex. Use a single person (🧍) for a survey question.      Ex. Use a group of people (🧑‍🤝‍🧑) for a statistical question.

    1. Survey Questions: These are questions we ask to gather information from individuals.

      1. Connect to their observations:

        1. Question A is an example of a Survey Question because it asks about one person (Max).

        2. A survey question is what we might ask a witness during an interview for a case.

        3. The person responding can only give one specific value as their answer. For example, Max cannot choose English and Math as his favorite class.

        4. Survey questions help us collect raw data about individuals.

    2. Statistical Questions: These are questions that require us to collect many observations before we can provide an answer.

      1. Connect to their observations:

        1. Question B is an example of a Statistical Question because it asks about a group of people (all 30 suspects).

        2. A statistical question is like investigating the whole school to get the full story about the Candy Culprit.

        3. We should anticipate that answers will vary (recall the term variability). For example, we don't expect every suspect to have the exact same favorite class.

        4. Statistical questions require collecting data from multiple individuals to answer.

        Enrichment or Extension: Connect to Data Views in CODAP Ask: Which type of question allows us to consider a single case-card in CODAP? Answer: Survey Questions Ask: Which type of question requires us to consider the whole case-table in CODAP? Answer: Statistical Questions Ask: Which type of question could be answered with data from a single case/row? Answer: Survey Questions Ask: Which type of question could be answered with the entire case-table? Answer: Statistical Questions

  14. Create a connection about how statistical questions can help us during the Candy Culprit investigation.

    1. Remind students what information has been provided by the Candy Culprit from the previous clues:

      1. Clue #1: Chocolate candy was taken.

      2. Clue #2: There is mention of a “high GPA.”

    2. As a class, come up with a statistical question related to Clue #1, and write it on the board. Sample answer: Which type of candy do suspects choose most often as their “favorite”?

    3. As a class, come up with a statistical question related to Clue #2, and write it on the board. Sample answer: What is the typical GPA for 8th grade students?

    Part 2: Creating Our Own Statistical Questions

  15. Place students into small groups of size 3 or 4. They will engage in a Peer Review activity that is guided by the Statistical Questions Peer Review handout (LMR_U1_L07_A).

    LMR_U1_L07_A

    STRATEGY: Randomly Assigning Students to Groups Prepare card groups prior to class.As students come into class, have them draw a card at random. Once class begins, they must find the other students that match their card’s theme. Students can stay in this grouping arrangement for the day, week, or activity. Sample cards using film and television characters:

    • Example 1: The Scooby Doo Gang [Shaggy, Fred, Vilma, Daphne].
    • Example 2: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles [Leonardo, Donatello, Raphael, Michelangelo].
    • Example 3: Avengers [Ironman, Thor, Captain America, Black Widow].
    • Example 4: Toy Story [Woody, Buzz Lightyear, Bo Peep, Mr. Potato Head].
    Sample cards using generic categories:
    • Example 1: Instruments [Guitar, Piano, Drums, Trumpet].
